DEPARTMENT at FOCUS FEATURES AT 65 BLEECKER STREET, 3rd FLOOR, NY, NY, 10012 or 100 UNIVERSAL CITY PLAZA BLDG. 9128-2, UNIVERSAL CITY, CA 91608
INT. CITY MORGUE - SACRAMENTO, CA - DAY

Languid. Dreamlike. A slow-moving camera pushes into a refrigeration room of a MORGUE. A DETECTIVE, TWO UNIFORMED COPS, a MORGUE attendant, and the CORONER, are on the periphery of frame. Their conversation is indiscernible. At the center of frame is a MAN'S BODY lying on a slab, half in, half out of the morgue refrigerator. We do not see his face. THE CAMERA APPROACHES. SLOW. Past the group of people in the room. It moves up and over the body. IMAGE IS UPSIDE DOWN. Then--- CAMERA RIGHTS ITSELF. We find ourselves looking squarely into the face of GARY WEBB: 42, handsome, masculine. Two bullet wounds to his face and head. WE SMASH CUT TO --- a kaleidoscope of jump cut images and footage from the War on Drugs, both familiar and distant-- (UNDER AND THROUGH
(2-10) STOCK FOOTAGE

TO PRESIDENT RICHARD NIXON opening the "War on Drugs"... PRESIDENT NIXON/FOOTAGE Drugs are public enemy number one-TO NANCY REAGAN delivering her "Just Say No" speech... TO DEA AGENTS SEIZING a mountain of hundred-dollar bills... TO PABLO ESCOBAR in jungle fatigues, next to a plane loading hundreds of pearlescent white bricks... PRESIDENT REAGAN/FOOTAGE (OVER)
TO A ROCK HOUSE IN THE NICKERSON GARDENS PROJECTS, WATTS, LA

... detritus and urban decay ... Powder cooked into rocks by MOTHERS, their KIDS playing with the vials around their feet. TO A LINE OF JUNKIE MOTHERS wrapping the rock house... TO LAPD GANG UNITS - helmets and truncheons - bracing a wallful of corner hooks, look-outs and pimps. TO AFRICAN-AMERICAN CORPSES - women, children trapped in cross-fire - lying akimbo in an LA playground. TO WATTS ON FIRE ... blocks of flames ... urban warfare.
EXT. FAUX SPANISH MANSION - MISSION VIEJO, CA - SIMULTANEOUS11 TITLE: JULY 15, 1995

A MAN walks up to the door: 37, longish ash-blond hair, ruddy handsome. Notebook in back pocket. GARY WEBB. Vibrant. Alive. Door opens on RONNY QUAIL, 40, nose collapsed from a lifetime of blow. LITTLE HOTTIE, 19, topless and G-string, in tow.
Who the fuck are you?
Gary Webb. San Jose Mercury-News.
What do you want?
You called me, remember? Story on government seizure of property of, uh, accused narcotics dealers-QUAIL (hands over heart) Yeah yeah that's me. The accused ... c'mon in...
INT. FAUX SPANISH MANSION
Quail walks Webb in. No furniture. Webb sniffs. Cleaning and paint equipment piled in the corner.
This the house they're taking?
KILL THE MESSENGER SALMON DRAFT- 7/16/13
I remain optimistic.
(checks his notebook) You said you were charged with conspiracy-Something's off. The light. Nothing's coming through the
windows. Because they're all covered in tin foil.
What's with the foil?
Signals, man. Blocks microwave and radio.
The girl rolls her eyes and - topless - crosses to the coffee machine. Webb can't stop looking at the girl's ass.
But what they don't seem to get is
they can't get me-Webb takes in the girl. All of her fantastic body.
What the hell're you doing?
Webb displays his left hand. A shiny ring.
Relax. Married.
So what. So am I.
Momentary tension. Then they share a look, an understanding, maybe a smile. Brothers ... and--
EXT/INT FAUX SPANISH MANSION - POOLSIDE - TIME JUMP
They've been walk-and-talking for a while. Lapping the pool.
My last house - this sweet pad in Laguna - charges didn't stick and the Feds still didn't give it back--
KILL THE MESSENGER SALMON DRAFT- 7/16/13
(looks at him quizzically, then--) But you did it. Did--?
Off the record -- you sold the dope.

You're missing the point. They took the freakin roof over my head. They enter the house, Quail begins to make coffee -WEBB Okay, so I gotta just ask -because it's what they say -- the stuff you buy with dope money -your house, car, whatever -- you lose it, because it was the crime that paid for it.

Did it, didn't do it, who gives a shit. They can't take a man's shelter. Anyway, they have to prove it, right? They didn't prove it. I wasn't convicted. Pause, then-WEBB Why is that? I mean, you don't exactly deny it.
Quail looking at him.
You believe in conspiracy theories, Gary?
I don't believe in conspiracy theories. Conspiracy, yes. If I believe it, there's nothing "theory" about it.
I like you.

A LOUD sharp sound. As if the DOOR was knocked down. Webb whirling as --- COPS, AGENTS, SHERIFFS pour around the corner, into the room, pointing weapons -COPS/AGENTS/SHERIFFS D.E.A.! ... Los Angeles Sheriff
Christ. Here we go again.
The girl screams and runs.
Get that bitch!! ... Down motherfuckers, down!!
A dozen AGENTS fan out. Some head upstairs. Some head into
the basement.
Quail gives Webb a funny smile.
We'll have to have that coffee
another time-Webb drifting backward toward the door, hands up: What, me?
Ronald J. Quail, we have a warrant for your arrest for distribution of cocaine, wire fraud-SHERIFF 1 (CONT'D) (to Webb) Who the fuck are you!? (muzzle against Webb's temple) Hold on --
Webb reaching for an ID. Someone screams "Hands!" And Webb is mid-air, dropped on ass and back. Handcuffed.
I'm a reporter--!
Shut the fuck up!
KILL THE MESSENGER SALMON DRAFT- 7/16/13
You don't know what you're messing with, man! AGENTS come back with an assault rifle, shotgun.
(to Webb) Where's the dope?
I'm the reporter.
Don't you guys get it? I knew you guys were coming, man-Sheriff 1 hands DEA Agent a radio. COP/RADIO It's a burn. Dope's gone.
Everything sanitized, fresh paint,
carpet. Like they knew-Webb -- cheek to floor -- looks across at Quail.
You did know.
The DEA Agent slams the cleaning equipment against the wall.
(to Webb) They're not listening. I am.
Kit-homes and American flags. The heart of the lower-middle.
EXT. WEBB'S HOUSE - LIESEL CT., SACRAMENTO - LATER THAT DAY 14

A local HOCKEY CREW - post game - and WIVES around Webb's pool. Still in their jerseys. Webb at the grill, knuckles raw, upper lip smeared with dried blood. And there's Ian, now 16. Spitting image of the old man. In a Jersey, too. And Sue - now 34, with their other two kids, ERIC, 12; and CHRISTINE, 9. AC/DC playing in the b.g.
(laughing at Webb's face) You are the picture of graceless and out-of-control, a danger to your kind.
The guy just had a shotgun in his ear. Give him a break. (to Webb) So how'd that feel?
Did he really tin foil his whole house?
(from across the yard; always listening) Tell them about the half naked girl. Was she old enough to drive?
Man, I wish I had your life. SUE Cuz you want to be married to me?
Cuz Gary's my idol. And yeah-(glancing at his own wife) That thing about you. You're a rock star, too-Uh huh--
Dave puts a beer in Ian's hand.
C'mon, check this, fellas. (leads them to--)
INT./EXT. WEBB'S HOUSE - GARAGE - CONTINUOUS
There's Webb's Honda Interceptor, hottest of motorcycles. And an old wheezy Triumph. In need of love and spare parts.
Awwright, Dad!
We'll rebuild it together.
Ian hugs Webb hard. Webb puts the kid in a headlock.
Infant day before yesterday.
Sue sticks her head in. Phone in hand.
Hey. Yeah you. Hotshot. (face falls at the sight of the second bike) Goddammit we talked about this!
I changed our mind. It's a two-year project. Minimum.
Webb tangos across the garage. Sue warding him off.
I've waited for this, you know? The perfect ride. S'been sitting up here in my head for fourteen years. My boy and me riding to the mountaintops and touching the sky.
Then I watch him, my kid, head over the horizon. KILL THE MESSENGER SALMON DRAFT- 7/16/13
God, that's beautiful.
Shut up, Bob. (to Ian) You ready for all that, Ian? To be full of shit like your dad?
Yeah. I wanna ride to the hills andSUE Touch the sky. I know. (she grabs Webb's nuts) Something happens to my kid on that thing I'll cut these off while you're sleeping.
(toasts with a pint of motor oil) And here's to the happy couple! Ain't life grand!
I thought you already cut them off.
Sue hands him Webb phone. Whoever was on the other end heard the whole thing.
It's the paper.
INT. WEBB'S HOME OFFICE / BASEMENT - CONTINUOUS

Webb heads down the basement steps to his office. It's a reporter's lair, a quasi-organized chaos of boxes and files. A slew of journalism awards, including a Pulitzer. Framed stories from the Kentucky Post and Cleveland Plain-Dealer. Dirty aquarium gurgles in the corner. Poster hangs over it: "If it's in the Kentucky Post, it's the truth".
INT. NEWSROOM - SAN JOSE MERCURY NEWS - SAN JOSE
INTERCUT ANNA SIMONS in the sprawling newsroom of the San Jose Mercury-News. Editor, pretty, 35, harried, unmarried. Alone with the weekend skeleton crew. KILL THE MESSENGER TAN PAGES 8/05/13
(sits at his computer) Is it up?
Goes to press in ten.
Webb opens email. Brings up galleys for a newspaper piece.
Where's the last graph, Anna?
We ran out of inches.

Cut the kicker you blow the whole point. The government takes away these people's shit, their houses, their cars, forever - before they're convicted. (silence) Even if they're acquitted they don't get it back.
They're drug dealers, Gary.
Not until they're convicted. Because this is America. That's the story.
Four minutes until deadline.
Take my name off the story.
(after a silence) They were right about you.
You're an asshole.
(contemplating the fish in the aquarium) Yes I am an asshole. About this. They were also right about you. What part?
First in her family to go to college, chip on her shoulder, ambitious, trying to make her working class parents proud.
Click. She hangs up.
INT. WEBB'S HOUSE - IAN'S BEDROOM - LIESEL CT. - NEXT MORNING

6:30am. Ian wakes, TRACK HIM stumbling down the hall in underwear, exchanging sleepy salutes with Sue, who's guiding Christine to the bathroom. And downstairs, past the couch-Where Webb is sleeping, clothed. Ian toes at him. And heads to the front door. Picks the Mercury-News off the stoop. Dad.
(Webb grunts) You ever gonna go back to your room?
Ask your mother.
It's none of his business. (to Ian) Your dad's in the doghouse and you will be too if you're late for school.
INT. WEBB'S HOUSE - KITCHEN - DAY
Sue starts doing eight things at once, kids' lunch, breakfast, dishes. Webb tries to help but mostly in the way.
(reading Webb's story; island of calm) Dad, you hang with some insanely bad people.
Funny thing. Bad guys are usually more honest than good guys. And more fun. It's an inside-out world. SUE Life lessons by Gary Webb.
Mom, you have to read this.
Sue says nothing. Webb plants a kiss on the back of her neck.
Gotta go gotta go gotta roll, munchkins!
Daddy's taking us?!
Train leaves in five ... four ...
INT./EXT. WEBB'S HOUSE - LIVING ROOM - LIESEL CT.
Sue watches the kids pour into the shitty little car. Fights back a smile. Then picks up the paper. And starts to read.
INT. NEWS BUREAU - SACRAMENTO - DAY - ESTABLISHING

Webb bangs around a square of hallways housing the California bureaus of the major national newspapers. Bustling and loud. Past the Los Angeles Times suite (huge, lots of REPORTERS); New York Times (plush and stately); Washington Post (beautiful and big). And into-- KILL THE MESSENGER BUFF PAGES- 7/21/13
INT. SAN JOSE MERCURY NEWS BUREAU - SACRAMENTO - SAME
The Merc. Cramped and thread-bare, country cousins to the majors. TOMMY FARRAGHER, 30's, doughy Irish guy. Webb's office has an actual door.
Yo. I know how bad you wanna do the Guv today. Real reporter work.
(grabbing a stack of pink 'While-you-were-outs') How that crap gives you satisfaction I'll never know.
Meat and potatoes, King Shit. Someone's gotta do it. (then) Hey, Fancy. That story this morning. That was a big one, son.
But not the "Big One".
Curious. Does the Big One have a
sound? Does it go 'woosh'?
It's like a bullet with your name on it. You never hear it coming. They look up. A young kid in his 20's is in the doorway. He heard that. Hey.
Boy Wonder, Rich Kline, L - A Times. What can I do for you?
Hardly Boy Wonder. My editor's pissed we missed that story.
He should be.
KILL THE MESSENGER BUFF PAGES- 7/21/13
Amazing job, by the way. Any advice for me?
Yeah, don't let the assholes win. (his phone rings) Gotta go, kid.
INT. WEBB'S OFFICE - MERC BUREAU - SACRAMENTO - SAME
The room a wreck of controlled chaos. Webb shuffling through the while-you-were-outs. Three from a "CORAL BACA".

(calls out there) Who the hell is Coral Baca and why does she keep calling me--? (dialing; into phone) This is Gary Webb. CORAL/PHONE He finally calls. Her accent Latina. Sultry. CORAL/PHONE (CONT'D) I like your work.
You follow my work? I do now.
CORAL/PHONE
What can I do for you?
It's what I can do for you.
Okay, what can you do for me?
Your story today. I thought you might be interested in a follow-up.
KILL THE MESSENGER BUFF PAGES- 7/21/13
You called me five times this morning for a follow-up?
Three times. I'm a woman. And I wanted something. (then) My boyfriend Raffie's in prison for cocaine trafficking. That's Rafael Cor-ne-jo.
(starts doodling) Colombian? CORAL/PHONE

Absentmindedly scrawling "Cornejo". Doodling around it. CORAL/PHONE (CONT'D) He has this gorgeous house the government just took. Twentythousand-dollar Italian couch I designed myself. Handmade wallpaper. Good story, no?
Yeah. I just wrote it.
Raffie's story is different. He sold drugs for the government.
(pencil freezes) Can you say that again?
He brought four tons of cocaine into the country. For the government.
Webb scrawls a big question mark, underlines it, circles it. Stares at it -- nah -- finally scrawls a thick X over it.
That's, um, a little-Crazy?
CORAL/PHONE KILL THE MESSENGER SALMON DRAFT- 7/16/13
That'll work.
You think I'm one of those conspiracy nut jobs, don'tcha?
You have a nice voice, Coral. Thanks for calling. (about to hang up--)
What if I can prove everything? I've copied every piece of paper. (Webb's hand pauses) Selling drugs for the government. Have you written that story? No.
Then maybe you should buy me lunch.
INT. DINER - SACRAMENTO - DAY
Odd hour of day, between meals. Mostly empty.
You must be Gary.
He turns. She's all cleavage and bejeweled fingers. Mid-20's. Raven hair, long legs, short skirt. The five-course meal.
And you're Coral. (they shake) How about those documents?
He sits. She reaches across, fingers the end of his hair.
What, no foreplay? Gary does not look at her tits. No.
Coral brings documents out of a briefcase: "CONFIDENTIAL PROPERTY OF US GOVT - MAY NOT BE REPRODUCED".
This is the thing you need to see. Wait. Wow.
IN TIGHT: "GRAND JURY FOR NORTHERN DISTRICT OF CALIFORNIA, TRANSCRIPT OF GRAND JURY TESTIMONY OF DANILO BLANDON".
Are you kidding me? A grand jury transcript? In fifteen years I've maybe seen one. How'd you get it?
The government turned it over on discovery by mistake. Russell Dodson.
The Federal prosecutor Russell Dodson?
That little bitch. I called the cops on him once.
You called the cops on the Feds?
His goons were sneaking around my house. Could have been rapists. (she smiles, and--)
I think I'm gonna like you, Coral. (wants to concentrate) Give me a minute-Not looking at her tits. Bent over the transcript, rapt.
You read all this? A little.
What's this they keep blacking out?
TRANSCRIPT: lots of redactions with thick black marker. Coral gets out of her booth, slides into Gary's booth next to him. She leans in, reaching into the pile of papers. Breasts flowing. Touching.
who deals for the government. (Webb reading) He was a friend. He played with my kid. Then he rats. Weirds me out.
(reading on) Government payroll. Selling dope. And ... lots of it.
She points again: "Norwin Meneses".
Blandon's boss. Norwin Meneses. He's probably the biggest trafficker in the whole country.
They couldn't be that stupid.
Oh but they are. The biggest dope dealers in the country. Way way bigger than Raffie. Why use whale to catch catfish? That make sense to you, Gary Webb?
Not very much sense no. What other documents do you have? She smells great. The tits are great. Webb's not gonna look.
Everything is at my house -He's not gonna look at her tits.
You wanna come over? (he looks; fuck) (MORE)
You want to make those part of the deal?
Just send the documents to my office.
First things first, Gary Webb. Raffie's got court tomorrow. Be there.
EXT. FEDERAL COURTHOUSE. SAN FRANCISCO. DAY - ESTABLISHINGA25
Webb makes his way into the courthouse.
INT. FEDERAL COURTHOUSE / GALLERY - SAN FRANCISCO - DAY
Webb heads up the steps of the courthouse gallery --
INT. FEDERAL COURTHOUSE / OUTSIDE OF COURTROOM - SAN FRANCISCO A26 CONTINUOUS
-- Webb approaches the court room. Outside the door of the court is Coral, dressed in a black little number suited for funerals and trials.
(to Webb) The judge called an early recess. (looks across the hallway) There's Raffie.
TWO FEDERAL MARSHALS escort Cornejo into a back room. He's handsome and in a fancy suit. He glares at Webb.
He looks friendly.
He doesn't like seeing me with other men. (no shit--; she checks her watch) Maybe this can get going --
Webb clocks a large blond MAN, 40, among a group of Federal prosecutors and a few expensive attorneys.
That's that little bitch. Dodson.
RUSSELL DODSON, 40, prom king, walks away from the group, and down the hall.
Be right back.
Webb follows him.
INT. MEN'S ROOM - FEDERAL COURT HOUSE - CONTINUOUS
Webb steps up into the urinal next to Dodson's.
Gary Webb, San Jose Mercury-News. Dodson shakes, re-zips, steps to the sinks.
You always follow people into bathrooms? No.
Why's local news interested in this case?
I'm not. But I am interested in
Never heard of him.
Then why's he on your witness list?

WEBB'S POV TIGHTENS on Dodson's face in the mirror. Eyes like cameras, notes every detail, tic at the mouth; missed a spot with the razor. Professional surveillance-- KILL THE MESSENGER SALMON DRAFT- 7/16/13
I have his grand jury transcript. (Dodson looks at him) Yep. I've seen screw-ups outta you guys, but that's a big one.
Dodson stares at him. Then exits.
EXT. FEDERAL COURTHOUSE STEPS - SAN FRANCISCO - DAY
FROM AFAR -- As is if they're being watched -- Webb and Coral on the courthouse steps. They're exchanging information. He shakes her hand. We follow Webb to a PARKING GARAGE --
INT. COURTHOUSE GARAGE/WEBB'S CAR - SAN FRANCISCO - DAY A28
--Webb gets into his car. As he reaches for the ignition-A hard knock on the passenger side glass. Dodson. Standing there. Webb reaches and opens the door. Dodson gets in. Sour look on his face.
You really think drug dealers deserve to hang onto the crap they buy with money they make off thirteen-year-old junkies?
Is that what you wanted to talk about? No.
(then) Tell me how this works. For real. She fucking you? You think she wouldn't fuck me if I let her scumbag boyfriend go?
That's not how it works.
Webb looks at him. Dodson is actually sweating. A spongy moistness across his forehead. Webb smells the bruise-- KILL THE MESSENGER BUFF PAGES- 7/21/13
So you have Danilo Blandon cold on what looks like major narcotics and laundering. Then just let him walk. Why's this guy showing up on witness lists? Why isn't he in a dungeon somewhere?
I hate you people.
Stop. Webb realizing, dawning on him-WEBB
You believe in redemption, Webb?
Only when I have to.
What will it take to keep Blandon out of your paper? I'll give you any other story, any one you want.
You're making me wanna know what I'd be giving up.
This is about being a good American. Do you know what it means to be a good American?
Truth and justice.
Where did you grow up? Indiana.
On a farm? (Webb nods) With animals? (MORE)
(Webb nods) Jesus. Is that really true?
Everything except the animals.
Well, there are more important things than the truth, even in Indiana. (beat; then) Even in Cleveland. STOP. Webb stiffens and that word. Cleveland.
(angry) We're done.
Good. Get the fuck out.
It's my car. Right. Dodson gets out.
I'm not going away. I'm a reality. Blandon's a reality.
Reality's classified!
He really said "reality's classified"? He know he was on the record?
I guess he forgot.
INT. WEBB'S OFFICE - MERC BUREAU - SACRAMENTO - DAY
Anna and Webb mid-conversation. Blandon grand jury transcript in her hands.
(nonplussed) So what's the story? (MORE)
Paranoid Justice official off his meds? Feds use bad guy to catch other bad guys? It's low-hanging fruit, Gary.
You have no idea what that is, do you? (the transcript)
I know what it is.
Well, it's unheard of. It's a gift. (and) I asked around about Blandon. No one's heard of him. Why is that?
Farragher sticks his head in the door.
Talking here--
Farragher bows ... "Sorry, chief" ... backs out. Anna waves the transcript. Unconvinced.
I've just never seen a suit like that so desperate.
Define desperate.
He brought up Cleveland.
He threatened you?
That's what I'm telling you. I'm a nobody. But he got himself educated in a minute flat. Whatever I got near, it made him freakin nervous. She's thinking about it. Hesitant.
I know what I saw. I've been doing this a long time.
Don't do old-and-wise. I hate that.
I'm not old.
And we're not the LA Times.
We're not small-time either.
Get Blandon and see if it's anywhere worth going. (and she's up and out)
INT. FOYER/KITCHEN - WEBB'S HOUSE - LIESEL CT. - EVENING

Later that evening, Gary enters the house. He throws his keys down in the foyer, walks into the kitchen. He stops at the sight of a banker's box -- "FOR GARY" in big loopy feminine script sitting like a turkey in the middle of the table. The kids around it eating fish sticks. Sue puts some more plates on the table.
Who's Coral? Shit. She's hot.
She came into my house.
She drove a Camaro.
Of course she did. SUE Why does she know where you live?
I don't know why. She's a source.
For a story about strippers?
Drug dealers. SUE Oh. Much better. (then) Is it happening again, Gary?
Is what happening again?
Webb grabs Sue by the arm and pulls her out of the kitchen --
INT. WEBB'S HOUSE - LIESEL CT. - BEDROOM
Sue sitting on the bed. Webb standing. We're mid-scene.
So Guatemalan drug dealers and guys
Nicaraguan drug dealers. SUE And I can't believe you gave that woman our address. I didn't.
I don't know if that's better or worse.
He pulls her in. She lets him.
We had an agreement. We're turning a new leaf. You get to look in all the drawers and closets now. Isn't that what we said? SUE What happened with that woman?
What happened with that woman?
KILL THE MESSENGER BUFF PAGES- 7/21/13 Nothing.
She grabs him by the arms.
Better not. (then) Let's not do this again. It's why we left my family, my friends, moved out to California, which I hate, f.y.i, all this stupid sunshine and happy shiny faces.
It's a new day. A Gary and Sue Webb sunrise.
I'm serious. So am I.
And you believe that woman?
I don't need to. I just need what's in that box. Sue looking at him. Watching him. Then-WEBB (CONT'D) I'm not gonna screw this up, baby.
She grabs Webb's hair. Pulls it. Hard.
You better not. Because you're the love of my goddamn life.
She steers his face toward hers. Where she can see straight into his eyes. Bores right into him.
She said to tell you there's another hearing tomorrow. Now kisses him. Hard. And then releases him. And then simply
walks away.
INT. WEBB'S HOME OFFICE - LATER THAT NIGHT

A rectangle of light spills out of Webb's home office. Inside is Webb, standing by a window. He smokes a joint, carefully blowing the smoke out the window. In front of him spread out on his desk, are the documents from the box.
INT. WEBB'S HOUSE - GARAGE - DAY
Ian, dressed for school, finds Webb still in his PJs. Been up all night. Standing over the Triumph -- disintegrated into two hundred pieces. A collage of machine guts.
When did you do this? Last night.
I thought we were doing it together.
I pulled at a wire to see what gauge we needed and kept going. (beat) You think you can put this back together and fix it?
I don't know.
Webb puts him in a head lock. Playful.
I know. Yes you can. You will.
Releases him, then walks him over to the motorcycle parts.
bearings. I know it looks like a
He grabs up an old TRIUMPH REPAIR MANUAL, gives it to Ian.
A road map to start. Then your instincts take over.
(fanning through the manual) What if we're missing parts?
Find new ones. (re; book) It's all in there. You're never alone.
Ian looks at him.
I want to ride this bike, dad. I want to ride it next to you.
You will. We will. Put it back together. (MORE)
Figure out how things work. (garage clock) I'm late.
Webb rushes out, leaves Ian standing over the parts. A daunting challenge.
INT. COURT ROOM - FEDERAL COURT HOUSE - SAN FRANCISCO - DAY 35

Webb enters on Raphael Cornejo's trial. A half dozen FAMILY MEMBERS of defendants scattered. Dodson and the DEFENSE ATTORNEYS are at sidebar with the judge. Dodson clocks Webb's entrance. Scowls. Leans into judge. Webb crosses to Coral in a back row. More cleavage, more jewelry, more wattage. She looks cheerful.
What's going on?
They've been up there for a while.
Dodson crosses back toward his table as-JUDGE
Very impressive, Gary Webb. What just happened?
You terrified them. They just stuffed Danilo Blandon under a rug. He's off the witness list. No Blandon, no case. Raffie's walks. One day maybe I can thank you properly.
She leaves the courtroom. Webb catches up with her -WEBB He grabs her arm. Cornejo glaring from inside the courtroom.
(knowing) You used me to get the case dropped.
All of her smiles. Her face. Her gorgeous body.
I'm just the bimbo, remember? (leans; whispers) Be happy, Gary Webb. You thought you were getting a piece of cheese. But I just gave you the mouse--
And nods toward Dodson, who is leading a wedge of U.S. MARSHALS hustling out DANILO BLANDON, a well-tailored Nicaraguan in his 30's, aviator glasses, healthy, like he lives at a spa.
There's your story. Danilo Blandon.
INT. WEBB'S OFFICE - MERC BUREAU - SACRAMENTO - NIGHT

Where Webb -- alone in the building -- is pouring over the documents Coral gave him. Been at it for hours. Surrounded by coffee cups, burger wrappers. Above his desk, he's taping stuff up now. Mug shot of Blandon. Surveillance shot of Meneses. Then STOP. Something he's looking at. FBI report. IN TIGHT: graph about the way Cornejo moved his cocaine.
